title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | House passes correspondence bill EDUCATION: The measure is intended to provide assurance to home-school parents. The Associated Press Wire (Published: May 4, 2002) Juneau -- A bill aimed at preventing the state Department of Education and Early Development from toughening rules for statewide correspondence schools passed the House on Friday. The measure would put into law key elements of a compromise set of regulations agreed to in March by the department and correspondence school administrators. The bill's sponsor, Rep. Jeannette James, R-North Pole, said the measure is intended to provide assurance to correspondence school parents that the department won't back out of the compromise. "We tried to put as much of the regulations into the bill as we could actually to keep them from chan…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Senate approves water study SPENDING: Vote paves the way for state to take over the federal waste permit program. The Associated Press Wire (Published: May 4, 2002) Juneau -- The Senate voted Friday to spend about $200,000 to study whether the state should take over a federal wastewater permit program. Alaska is one of six states that does not issue the federally required pollution permits at the state level, said Tom Chapple of the Department of Environmental Conservation. Municipal sewage plants, seafood plants, mining, oil and gas and other industrial operations must have the permits to operate. Chapple said there could be advantages to Alaska taking over the program. "The most obvious thing is it brings decision-making to Alaska rather than Seattle," Chapple said. "It would on its …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Friends of Pets, Pet Patrol celebrate successful alliance ADOPTIONS: Organizers highlight importance of responsible pet ownership. By Rose Cox Anchorage Daily News (Published: May 4, 2002) Animal rights proponent Diane Raynor counts instigating Pet Patrol as one of her better ideas. "It's one of the most important things I've done," Raynor said. Pet Patrol has included a photo of an adoptable pet in the Anchorage Daily News weekly for the past decade, resulting in the adoption of hundreds of abandoned animals through the all-volunteer organization Friends of Pets. "We have no idea of the total impact Pet Patrol has had, because there have been so many additional adoptions that have come as a result of an animal being featured in the paper," said Michele Girault of Friends of Pets. "For…

title: The Anchorage Daily News (www.adn.com)
summary: Lower Kalskag POPULATION: 267 (December 2001) LOCATION: On the north bank of the Kuskokwim River, 2 miles downriver from Upper Kalskag. It lies 26 miles west of Aniak, 64 miles northeast of Bethel and 350 miles west of Anchorage. DESCRIPTION: An Eskimo village of Russian Orthodox practitioners who relocated from Upper Kalskag in 1940. More than 95 percent of population is Alaska Native or part Native. Subsistence activities provide food and the economic base. Salmon, moose, black bear, porcupine and waterfowl are utilized. Year-round employment is limited to the school, city government and clinic. BLM fire fighting can provide seasonal income. There are two schools, attended by about 135 students. HISTORY: The site was originally used as a fish camp for families of Upper Kalskag. In 1930, …

title: Town centers don't fit reality
summary: Reality rules. That is a basic concept in real estate development. While there can be grand plans, a project can go forward only if it meets all the basic elements for success. The proposed Abbott town center development in South Anchorage has run smack into reality. The final plan is drastically smaller than and different from the original vision. It also requires government financial support, which may not be available. This reality prompts the question: Is the town center plan best for Anchorage? The city wants Anchorage's growth to be of better quality, to avoid repeating mistakes made in the past here as well as in other cities. Our growth so far has been somewhat haphazard in many ways. We can certainly do better. To this end, the city has adopted a long-term land use and development…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | City had pulled two water tanks SHORTAGE: 2 reservoirs were not in service as mayor urged conservation. By Peter Porco Anchorage Daily News (Published: May 25, 2002) At the same time the city water supply to South Anchorage grew so short that Mayor George Wuerch was calling on people to conserve, the city had pulled two of its six local reservoirs out of service for maintenance. Stop watering yards and washing cars, the mayor urged South Anchorage residents on Tuesday. He said the city could solve the problem with a bigger waterline but is waiting on federal environmental agencies for permits. The six giant city water tanks south of Tudor Road serve as a buffer, meeting periods of high demand and refilling during low demand. Two of them have been out of service for routine maintenance …

title: Scientists seek orca, sea lion link
summary: Kodiak -- The Alaska Killer Whale Count is a new project being launched this summer by researchers, and they're asking for help. Andrew Trites, a well-known marine mammal biologist at the University of British Columbia, is traveling around the state to promote the unique research effort. He is also updating people on the latest research findings in a 45-minute presentation called "Steller sea lion declines -- Overfishing? Killer whales? Environmental change?" In the past, Trites' has focused on nutritional studies; specifically, the role that pollock plays in the sea lion diet. His years of research have revealed that pollock is like "junk food" to the marine mammals. Now, Trites said more people are beginning to look very closely at predation on sea lions by killer whales. "We know mathem…

title: State jumps in income growth
summary: Alaska ranked fourth best among the states for personal income growth in 2001, according to new figures from the U.S. Department of Commerce. That's a big turnaround from two years ago, when Alaska came in dead last. In 2001, a combination of renewed vigor in Alaska's oil and construction industries, coupled with the Lower 48 recession and dot-com collapse, made Alaska and some other energy-producing states look good, said economists who track the state. The federal Bureau of Economic Analysis ranked New Mexico, Wyoming, Oklahoma and Alaska as the leading states for per capita personal income growth in 2001. Alaska's growth rate was 4.6 percent, compared to 2.7 percent for the nation. Bringing up the rear was Washington state, which had a growth rate of 1.1 percent. Other bottom-enders wer…

title: Welton made boyfriend insurance trustee
summary: Suzette Welton's boyfriend told jurors Tuesday he was unaware Welton made him trustee of life insurance policies she took out on her two sons months before the youngest was killed in a fire. Michael Minzlaff said during Welton's murder trial in Palmer Superior Court that he didn't know about the arrangement until a state trooper told him after the September 2000 blaze that killed 14-year-old Samuel. Prosecutors say Welton, 38, plotted to kill her sons Samuel and Jeremiah, now 18, to collect the insurance money. With debts piling up, they say, she drugged the teens, then set fire to the family's Wasilla apartment. Welton also is charged with attempted murder and arson. Defense attorneys argue that Welton bought $100,000 life insurance policies as an investment for her sons. They say one of …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Alaska gathers more than $40 million in federal aid By Sarana Schell Anchorage Daily News (Published: May 7, 2002) The federal dollars continue to pour in. Alaska's congressional delegation announced more than $40 million in grants and loans were awarded to businesses and agencies in the state during the past week. A sampling: Anchorage's international airport was alloted $5 million from the U.S. Department of Transportation. The annual payment is part of a 10-year FAA grant to fund expansion and other improvements, said Dave Eberle, regional director, state Department of Transportation. The Matanuska-Susitna Borough will get $1.3 million for wastewater system improvements. The University of Alaska Fairbanks received a $1.5 million grant to "bolster long-term value of Alaska's marine r…

title: Southeast elders are recognized as cultural treasures
summary: Juneau -- Their knowledge is priceless. Their minds contain a wealth of Tlingit, Haida and Tsimshian songs, stories, memories and protocol. And now 12 elders around Southeast Alaska officially can call themselves "treasures." The Central Council Tlingit and Haida Indian Tribes of Alaska honored its first-ever "Living Cultural Treasure" designees in Juneau last month, an event that grew from one woman's determination, many communities' appreciation and the lifetime achievements of those receiving the awards. "This gives us an honor; we are recognized, finally," said Clara Peratrovich of Klawock, one of the elders to receive the designation. "It shows that the young generation values their culture." Volunteer project organizer Norma Jean Dunne started the push to honor the elders as a colleg…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Firm to steam ahead with study of ferry system MCDOWELL GROUP: The company is taking a look at improving management. The Associated Press (Published: May 24, 2002) Juneau -- A private organization is going ahead with a study on improving the management of the ferry system even though the Legislature recently found $20 million to restore the Marine Highway Fund. "That extra $20 million won't last them too long," said Loren Gerhard, executive director of the Southeast Conference, a nonprofit advocate for transportation needs in the Panhandle. The conference just released a preliminary draft of a $30,000 study by the McDowell Group of Juneau and Anchorage, a research and consulting firm. The study, due for completion in July, will be used to attract federal funding for a comprehensive ana…

title: House OKs health care pool' plan
summary: Juneau -- The House approved a bill Tuesday aimed at lowering insurance costs for small businesses, self-employed individuals and nonprofit groups. The bill would have the state help set up an insurance pool that the small entities could join. Rep. Norm Rokeberg, R-Anchorage, said by joining together into a larger pool, the groups may be able to get a better deal on insurance than they could alone. He is sponsoring the bill. "They need some glue to pull them together, a conduit to pull them together," Rokeberg said. The state would fill that role by forming the pool and seeking bids from private insurance companies, he said. Rokeberg said about 16,000 Alaskans -- or about 19 percent -- may be without health insurance, partly because the price is out of reach for small businesses. And the m…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Interior fires threaten homes, near resort EVACUATIONS: Residents flee Chena River subdivision as crews work. By Zaz Hollander Anchorage Daily News (Published: May 25, 2002) Jason Gaynes, Josh Leutzinger and Jon Glover from the Mat-Su Alaska State Forestry fire team work on putting out hot spots on a small brush fire off Abbott Loop Road on Friday. (Photo by Bob Hallinen / Anchorage Daily News) Fire officials said this Memorial Day weekend could be a hot one. They're right so far. The most significant of five major fires burning around the state threatened homes in a subdivision at the end of Chena Hot Springs Road, northeast of Fairbanks. By Friday, the fire had destroyed at least one 2,500-square-foot cabin and caused the closure of the road about seven miles short of the popular Che…

title: ACLU appeals gay benefits case to Supreme Court
summary: The Alaska Civil Liberties Union appealed a lawsuit Wednesday to the state Supreme Court in an effort to get benefits extended to gay couples working for the state or Municipality of Anchorage. Nine gay or lesbian employees and their partners have joined the ACLU in the suit that goes back to 1999, shortly after Alaska voters passed a constitutional amendment barring state recognition of gay marriage. The ACLU expects the state's highest court to hear the case this year. Plaintiffs argue that the state's ban on gay marriage makes it impossible for gays working for the state or city to get benefits for their partners because marriage is required to qualify. "It is patently unconstitutional for a state to say that you have to be married in order to get benefits, while at the same time denyin…

title: Nathanson surrenders his license
summary: In the midst of an ongoing state investigation into his professional practices, Dr. Steven Nathanson surrendered his medical license this week and ended more than 20 years of work in Alaska. "The whole thing has been sort of a sadness for all of us because he's a very nice person," said Dr. Keith Brownsberger, a member of the state medical board and an Anchorage internist. "But his judgment on many things was poor." Five of the eight members of the Alaska State Medical Board, including Brownsberger, talked via conference call Friday morning. The board unanimously approved Nathanson's voluntary surrender, said Gary Veres, chief investigator for the Division of Occupational Licensing. Howard Lazar, Nathanson's attorney, could not be reached for comment. Nathanson's office voice mail message,…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Welton made boyfriend insurance trustee CHARGES: Michael Minzlaff testifies in woman's murder trial. By Nicole Tsong Anchorage Daily News (Published: May 8, 2002) Suzette Welton's boyfriend told jurors Tuesday he was unaware Welton made him trustee of life insurance policies she took out on her two sons months before the youngest was killed in a fire. Michael Minzlaff said during Welton's murder trial in Palmer Superior Court that he didn't know about the arrangement until a state trooper told him after the September 2000 blaze that killed 14-year-old Samuel. Prosecutors say Welton, 38, plotted to kill her sons Samuel and Jeremiah, now 18, to collect the insurance money. With debts piling up, they say, she drugged the teens, then set fire to the family's Wasilla apartment. Welton also …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | House OKs health care pool' plan INSURANCE: Bill attempts to cut costs by pulling groups together By Cathy Brown The Associated Press (Published: May 8, 2002) Juneau -- The House approved a bill Tuesday aimed at lowering insurance costs for small businesses, self-employed individuals and nonprofit groups. The bill would have the state help set up an insurance pool that the small entities could join. Rep. Norm Rokeberg, R-Anchorage, said by joining together into a larger pool, the groups may be able to get a better deal on insurance than they could alone. He is sponsoring the bill. "They need some glue to pull them together, a conduit to pull them together," Rokeberg said. The state would fill that role by forming the pool and seeking bids from private insurance companies, he said. Roke…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Firing range, drilling permit bill passes WASTES: Measure would reverse decision by Alaska Supreme Court. By Maureen Clark The Associated Press (Published: May 15, 2002) Juneau -- The House passed a bill Tuesday that would undo a unanimous Alaska Supreme Court decision on permitting requirements for oil drilling wastes in Cook Inlet. The bill also would exempt firing ranges from state waste disposal permitting requirements. The measure, sponsored by Sen. Gene Therriault, R-North Pole, was a response to two separate lawsuits filed by environmental groups. It passed the House by a 34-3 vote. The bill, which passed the Senate Friday, now goes to Gov. Tony Knowles. "The governor wants a full review of this bill from his agencies before deciding what action he'll take," Knowles' spokesman B…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Old airliner reincarnated for Bush detail MAKEOVER:Mechanics restore vintage Douglas DC-6 to carry fuel. By Mary Beth Smetzer Fairbanks Daily News-Miner (Published: May 20, 2002) Fairbanks -- A middle-age makeover, complete with a nose job, is giving a lift to an aging workhorse with a new role. The 44-year-old Douglas DC-6 has been carefully reconfigured by a team of 40 workers so it can take on a new role of carrying fuel to the Bush. Mechanics have spent the past 14 months restoring and refitting the four-engine veteran to Federal Aviation Administration specifications. The plane's captain, Ron Klemm, added patriotic nose art and rechristened the flying ship The Spirit of America. "It's a sign of the times," Klemm said, saying the Sept. 11 attacks inspired the name and a furled port…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Community datebook (Published: May 2, 2002) BLOOD BANK Blood Bank of Alaska has ongoing needs. Donations can be made 10 a.m.-6 p.m. Monday through Saturday at 4000 Laurel St. Appointments are recommended at 563-3110 in Anchorage, 1-907-260-5672 in Soldotna and 1-907-376-1195 in Wasilla. Current needs include: Condition Red (one-day supply): A-negative. Condition Yellow (two-day supply): O-positive, O-negative. Condition Green (three-day or more supply): A-positive, B-positive, B-negative, AB-positive, AB-negative. SCHOOL LUNCH TODAY Anchorage: Elementary -- bean burrito or roast chicken with mashed potatoes and gravy or corn dog, diced pears, carrot sticks, chocolate chip cookie, milk. Middle school -- egg roll with Oriental rice with additional entrees offered, fruit and vegetable, as…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Community Profile (Published: May 2, 2002) Akiak POPULATION: 309 (December 2001) LOCATION: On the west bank of the Kuskokwim River, 42 air miles northeast of Bethel, on the Yukon-Kuskokwim Delta. DESCRIPTION: An Eskimo village with a reliance on subsistence and fishing activities, where about 95 percent of the population is Alaska Native. The majority of the employment in Akiak is with the city, school (about 100 students) and other public services. Commercial fishing and BLM firefighting also provide seasonal income; 27 residents hold commercial fishing permits. The community is interested in developing a fish processing plant and tourism. Poor fish returns since 1997 have significantly affected the community. HISTORY: In 1880, the village of "Ackiagmute" had a population of 175. The …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Alaska Digest (Published: April 25, 2002) PALMER Teen who died in blaze was worried, older brother testifies in murder trial Just hours before he died in a fire at his home, 14-year-old Samuel Welton told his older brother that he had a feeling something was going to go wrong that night, according to testimony Wednesday in a Palmer murder trial. Still Jeremiah Welton told jurors he doesn't believe his brother set the blaze on Sept. 15, 2000. Welton's mother, Suzette Welton, 38, is accused of setting the fire. Prosecutors believe she drugged her sons in hopes they would die in the blaze and she could collect on $100,000 life insurance policies she took out on them. Defense attorneys contend one of the teens set the blaze -- either accidently or deliberately. Jeremiah Welton, now 18, t…

title: Search articles for...
summary: In Alaska Outdoors News Let the season begin By Craig Medred Anchorage Daily News (Published: April 21, 2002) Kahiltna Glacier -- The mighty Chinook helicopters -- twin-rotor boxcars of the sky -- appeared small and silent far below, rising like tiny insects out of the abyss. Rescue team members move an outhouse stall to the base camp on Kahiltna Glacier. About 17,000 pounds of supplies were hauled to the mountain last week. (Photo by Jim Lavrakas / Anchorage Daily News) The enormity of the Mount McKinley massif was writ in the comparative insignificance of man's best technology. "Here they come," someone said, as the CH-47s swung east toward the site of the soon-to-be Kahiltna base camp and continued their climb. Chief climbing ranger Roger Robinson and Staff Sgt. Jason Wainwright stood o…

title: Bill would cut energy subsidies
summary: Juneau -- Subsidies paid for the high cost of power in some rural communities would be reduced under a bill passed Wednesday in the Senate. The bill changes the formula used to assist about 77,600 rural energy customers, many of whom still rely on expensive diesel generators for their electricity. About $2.9 million in annual funding from the state's Power Cost Equalization program could be eliminated with the formula change, passed on a party-line 14-6 vote in the GOP-controlled Senate. Democratic Gov. Tony Knowles is opposed to the measure, said Knowles spokesman Bob King. "We have serious concerns regarding the PCE bill passed out of the Senate. It raises everybody's bill," King said. The state began subsidizing rural power in Alaska in 1985 because of the high cost of electricity there…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Flu outbreak emptying schools VIRUS: Types A and B strike city; classroom absentee rates soar. By Ann Potempa Anchorage Daily News (Published: April 24, 2002) School nurse Gail White knew something was wrong April 15 when the Goldenview Middle School secretary needed extra paper to list all the students calling in sick. Then, student after student passing through White's office the same day complained of headache, sore throat, cough, stuffy nose and fever. On average, about 50 out of 900 students miss school daily at Goldenview. But on April 15, 132 kids were out. The next day, the number leaped to 140. A 12-year veteran of student sniffles and sneezes, White was concerned enough to pick up the phone. "This is the first time in my career," she said, "when I felt the need to call epid…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Rescue fee blatantly unfair to Mount McKinley climbers Craig Medred Outdoors (Published: April 21, 2002) Drop your snowmobile through a crevasse on the Spencer Glacier and the Alaska National Guard comes to your rescue with a helicopter from Anchorage. The cost? Nothing. Snowboard yourself into trouble in the far southeast corner of Wrangell-St. Elias National Park and Preserve and the guard is there again to save the day. Cost to the rescued? Nothing. Run your boat aground on some rocks off the southern tip of the Kenai Peninsula and the U.S. Coast Guard will arrive to help either by boat from Homer or helicopter from Kodiak. The cost of this service? Nothing. Why is it then that the climbers who head for Mount McKinley have to pay a $150-a-head rescue fee? I am not one of those peo…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Southeast elders are recognized as cultural treasures HONOR: Communities show appreciation while they still can. By Andrew Krueger The Juneau Empire (Published: May 2, 2002) Juneau -- Their knowledge is priceless. Their minds contain a wealth of Tlingit, Haida and Tsimshian songs, stories, memories and protocol. And now 12 elders around Southeast Alaska officially can call themselves "treasures." The Central Council Tlingit and Haida Indian Tribes of Alaska honored its first-ever "Living Cultural Treasure" designees in Juneau last month, an event that grew from one woman's determination, many communities' appreciation and the lifetime achievements of those receiving the awards. "This gives us an honor; we are recognized, finally," said Clara Peratrovich of Klawock, one of the elders …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Senate rejects state history class SCHOOLS: House added requirement to bill, and senators want it taken out. The Associated Press (Published: May 3, 2002) Juneau -- The Senate on Thursday blocked an attempt to require Alaska high school students to learn Alaska history. The House had put the history requirement into an unrelated Senate bill sponsored by Sen. Gene Therriault, R-North Pole, that dealt with school attendance. Therriault urged his fellow senators to reject the change the House made in his bill. "I think we should leave it up to the students, up to the districts," he said. Requiring districts to teach Alaska history would be an unfunded mandate, he added. Rep. Mary Kapsner, D-Bethel, sponsored the Alaska history bill, and it was passed in the House unanimously in February…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Ruling places blame on housing authority HOME FIRE: High court jury rules in favor of former Fort Yukon woman. The Associated Press (Published: May 3, 2002) Fairbanks -- A Superior Court jury has ruled in favor of a former Fort Yukon homeowner who charged her housing authority with being responsible for a 1995 house fire that caused the death of her 3-year-old son. Jurors returned a verdict Wednesday in the retrial of Denise James' civil lawsuit against the Interior Regional Housing Authority. The case was decided largely in James' favor in a 1997 trial before it was overturned on appeal. James, now living in North Pole, was awarded $480,000 in damages in the first trial, money she will now receive as long as the result of the retrial stands through the appeals process. James' Fort Y…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Kenai schools pinched CUTBACKS: Drop in state funding, lower enrollments likely to cost 26 teaching positions. By Jon Little Anchorage Daily News (Published: May 6, 2002) Next school year, Kenai Middle School's art program will be axed, along with reading and math tutoring. Kids will have a harder time getting into electives such as drama and home economics. And class sizes across the board will grow. Until now, principal Paul Sorenson could cobble together enough money to keep the programs humming, but he said a drop in state funding brought on by lower enrollment now leaves him helpless. "This time around, I'm not going to be able to hide those cuts," he said. "They're going to feel them from class offering to class size. These are going to be felt." Kenai Middle School, which will…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Crude signs Geologist Ken Bird searches for oil in remote NPR-A By Tony Hopfinger Anchorage Daily News (Published: May 12, 2002) Ken Bird doesn't work for an oil company but the government geologist has spent a quarter-century rocking out to help score Alaska's next big oil discovery. The U.S. Geological Survey employee has spent much of his time studying a slice of the Alaska Arctic the size of Indiana that until recently was known more as a forbidding land of broken dreams than a promising oil-hunting ground. Government and private oil explorers have come and gone over the decades at the National Petroleum Reserve-Alaska. But Bird has remained a fixture, roaming the 37,000 square miles in search of crude signs. "NPR-A is a fascinating place because there is so little known about it…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Inside Alaska Business (Published: May 3, 2002) Women's Yellow Pages goes online An old standard has gone high-tech: The Alaska Women's Yellow Pages is online for the first time this spring. The Alaska Women's Resource Center has published the directory of women-owned businesses and services for 13 years. Also, Alaska Women's Network has maintained a Web site with information on nonprofits, women-owned bed-and-breakfasts, meeting notices and other topics of interest to Alaska women. The Alaska Women's Yellow Pages, at www.alaskawomensnetwork.org , blends the two efforts. The online version is also the only version this year, said Barbara Henderson at AWRC. Henderson said competition for advertisers has cut into the project's profit, making printing too expensive. APU launches new bus…

title: School Board approves $454 million budget
summary: The Anchorage School Board on Wednesday unanimously approved superintendent Carol Comeau's proposed $454.9 million budget for the fiscal year beginning July 2002. Board members heard more than an hour of public testimony Wednesday night on community schools, riflery programs and middle school interscholastic basketball before mulling, then defeating, several amendments at the budget's second and final reading. The spending plan now goes to the Anchorage Assembly for possible revision and approval in March. Board member Tim Steele proposed several amendments that included hiring two community schools employees and adding half-time teachers' aids to every full-day kindergarten classroom. Other board members supported the ideas but were wary about unbalancing a budget they say spends money wh…
